items.question: What is the difference between one-hot and ordinal encoding?
items.answer: One-hot creates a binary column per nominal category level so no false order is imposed. Ordinal encoding maps ordered levels to integers 0..K−1 when rank is meaningful (e.g. low < medium < high). Use one-hot for unordered names; ordinal only when the step between levels is conceptually similar.

items.question: When would you use target encoding instead of one-hot?
items.answer: When cardinality is high (thousands of cities, merchants, or device ids) and full one-hot would be too sparse and memory-heavy. Target encoding replaces each level with a leakage-safe, smoothed target statistic—typically out-of-fold means on the training set—not the in-sample mean of all rows including the current one.

items.question: What is the main pitfall of target encoding?
items.answer: Label leakage: computing category means using the same row's target makes the feature a proxy for the label, inflating validation metrics. Fix with K-fold or leave-one-out statistics on train only, smoothing toward the global mean for rare categories, and never using validation/test labels when fitting encodings.

items.question: How should you handle unseen categories at inference?
items.answer: Decide at training time: sklearn `handle_unknown='ignore'` (all-zero vector), map to an explicit `__unknown__` or `other` bucket, or group rare levels before fitting. Document the policy—silent all-zero rows are not equivalent to a reference category unless designed that way.
